core: fast sink accepts one policy alert and records a delivered admin entry
duplicate alert idempotency keys produce one accepted delivery and one duplicate record
full queue with dead_letter records a dead-lettered alert with queue depth and capacity evidence
full queue with fail_closed returns 503 and stores a blocked receipt
receipts include alert_sink status, queue depth, queue capacity, delivery attempt counts, and terminal counts
advanced: slow sink keeps request latency bounded while delivery remains queued
fail_then_recover sink records two attempts and eventually delivers after retry
property_tests: generated duplicate idempotency keys are accepted once and duplicate thereafter

limitations: alert sink state is in-memory only and resets with the supervised process
slow sink behavior is simulated by delayed queue drain rather than an external network sink
graceful shutdown and drain semantics are not modeled beyond queue/admin visibility
delivery attempt history is inspectable through receipts and /admin/policy-alerts but is not persisted durably

notes: changing full queue on_full from dead_letter to drop would fail the dead-letter receipt/admin assertions
removing idempotency tracking would fail the duplicate example and generated property
making slow sink drain synchronously would fail the latency budget assertion
removing retry behavior would fail the fail_then_recover attempt-count assertion

self_review: The implementation is intentionally scoped to the Elixir prototype and does not touch Go or Rust files.
The request path calls a bounded GenServer enqueue and never waits for slow-sink delivery.
Receipts expose both per-alert enqueue results and aggregate queue/terminal counters.
The prototype favors contract observability over production durability; durable alert storage remains future work.
